page: Amazon Web Services c7i.metal-24xl is a bare-metal, compute-optimized server featuring a dedicated Intel Xeon 8488C processor. Operating on the x86_64 architecture, it delivers 48 physical cores and 96 virtual CPUs (vCPUs) with a base clock speed of 3.2 GHz. The processor is equipped with a 105 MB L3 cache, 2 MB L2 cache, and 48 KB L1d cache. It includes 192.0 GB of system memory, yielding a memory-to-core ratio of 2.0 GB per core. This bare-metal instance does not run a hypervisor and lacks local storage and GPU accelerators, relying instead on network-attached storage and a baseline network bandwidth of 38 Gbps.

Benchmark data indicates strong multi-core processing capabilities, with the server achieving top-tier (top 10%) performance in Geekbench multi-core, software compilation with Clang, and Passmark CPU tests. The Intel Xeon processor's advanced instruction sets, including AVX-512 and Advanced Matrix Extensions (AMX), contribute to top-tier results in extended instructions and encryption tests. Single-core performance is moderate, placing in the middle 50% for stress-ng single-core tests but reaching the top 25% in Geekbench single-core and single-threaded gzip compression. Memory performance is mixed; cached small-block read-only memory bandwidth ranks in the top 10%, whereas the overall Passmark memory score falls in the middle 50%.

Given its high compute density and lack of hypervisor overhead, the c7i.metal-24xl is suited for compute-intensive workloads such as software compilation, image processing, and text processing, where it demonstrates top-tier efficiency. It is also capable of handling medium-scale database operations and static web serving, where it registers strong performance. While it supports basic LLM inference, text generation speeds for larger models (such as 70B parameters) drop to average levels, indicating a tradeoff when using CPU-only architectures for large-scale AI generation. From a cost-efficiency perspective, the bare-metal configuration maximizes hardware resource utilization by eliminating virtualization overhead, making it a viable option for high-throughput parallel processing tasks that do not require local SSD storage or dedicated GPU hardware.
description: The Amazon Web Services c7i.metal-24xl is a bare-metal, compute-optimized server powered by a dedicated 96-vCPU Intel Xeon 8488C processor with a 3.2 GHz clock speed and 192.0 GB of RAM. Lacking a hypervisor and local storage, this x86_64 instance provides direct access to physical hardware, delivering 38 Gbps of baseline network bandwidth. Benchmark results highlight top-tier performance in multi-core processing, software compilation, and extended instruction execution, supported by integrated AVX-512 and AMX instruction sets. While single-core performance and large-block memory operations remain in the average to strong ranges, the server excels in parallelized tasks. It is highly suited for compute-heavy workloads such as software compilation, text and image processing, and static web serving, offering a virtualization-free environment that maximizes raw CPU resource density.
og_description: Amazon Web Services c7i.metal-24xl is a bare-metal, compute-optimized server featuring 96 vCPUs, 192 GB RAM, and a 3.2 GHz Intel Xeon processor, delivering top-tier multi-core performance.
meta_description: Amazon Web Services c7i.metal-24xl is a bare-metal server with 96 vCPUs, 192 GB RAM, and a 3.2 GHz Intel Xeon CPU, optimized for compute workloads.
tagline: A bare-metal, compute-optimized instance delivering ninety-six dedicated vCPUs and advanced Intel matrix extensions for high-throughput parallel processing.
bullet_points: Dedicated Intel Xeon 8488C processor with 96 vCPUs and 192 GB RAM.
Bare-metal architecture eliminating hypervisor overhead for direct hardware access.
Top-tier performance in multi-core benchmarks, software compilation, and text processing.
Integrated AVX-512 and AMX instruction sets for accelerated cryptography and matrix operations.
Baseline network bandwidth of 38 Gbps with no local storage.
Suited for parallel compute workloads, software builds, and static web serving.
